author: @wirlinfarseer2621
text:
Springs are just steel wire coiled...  Heat to glowing red, drop in water...poof it is Hardened.  This makes it a little more brittle though... so now you just gently heat it to a lower temperature and allow it to cool slowly in still air... Called Tempering.  

Making it now slightly less hard than just Hardened Steel, but a lot less brittle.

Can cool(Temper phase) it in a Carbon rich environment for a harder shell with a softer more bendable core...not so good for springs. <-- Carburizing
Or cool(Temper phase) it in a Nitrogen rich environment for a more durable spring.  <--- Nitriding

Hardened and Tempered Paper Clip springs would work fine for this level of force, imo.

author: @SianaGearz
text: A compromise with purging rubber and steel components ("vitamins") is that printed alternatives rarely measure up on endurance and quality. What helps is parametric design so you can substitute parts that you have access to, and adjust the model. I also think plumbing O-rings are sort of universal across the globe so those are OK. Getting the exact correct springs that an original design calls for can be really difficult though.

author: @Audio_Simon
text: I like your spring. When I design a PLA flex part, I never know how much material to use for the flex or travel I want.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: neither do i jus tdo it by trial and error - the thicker it is the stiffer it is

replies.author: @sierraecho884
replies.text: You do it with iterations. Print 2 different designs, one stiffer the other less stiff, than choose the one which is closer to what you need. Then print again 2 designs, chose the one closer to your needs and keep going until you end up with the design you want.

author: @realorfake4765
text: Regarding the springs: I found a video that talks about salt annealing. You put the 3d printed object into a pan full of salt and heat at 200C for 1 hour and then let it cool down over night. It's worth taking a look at.

author: @sierraecho884
text: The AD5X is a multi material printer wich can also act as an multi color printer, just like the Bambu lab etc. The only FDM multi color printer I know, which isn´t a multi material printer is the Davinci Color.

text: Would a coned popped seal better?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

replies.author: @sierraecho884
replies.text: @ You can also print the valve as an inverse cone and keep the seal flat. Basically like a upside down U.

author: @EricFranklin-d2v
text:
Excellent. A couple of questions: Can you make tpu mechanically bond to a more durable, heat and water resistant filament like ASA?

Could the spring be designed to print in a TPU type of material, and be printed in the same job with four changes between the two filaments, creating a single print as used valve?(Overhangs might be a problem)

And third, can we make an air compressor with those valves? Good stuff!

replies.author: @ecatVids
replies.text: I'm not sure about the all-in-one print, but a TPU-based spring element would certainly be neat.

replies.author: @ronnetgrazer362
replies.text: You can mechanically bond any materials by having many tiny reverse cone-shaped protrusions from one material embedded into the other material. In practice I think you're mushing the interface into a marbled batter, a bit like friction welding. Not as strong as a chemical bond of course, but it gets the job done most of the time for most of the makers. I think CNC kitchen did a thing about it.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Yeah you could but there will be a balance between that and time taken to print and wastage of material - you could do a spring in TPU for sure and most air compressors just use leaf valves don't they?
